title: Pull-apart dinner rolls
subtitle: Soft rolls baked in a touching tray
excerpt: Lightly enriched white dinner rolls proved and baked touching on a tray so each roll pulls apart with a soft, fluffy side. Eight rolls, one tin, twenty minutes in the oven.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — the dinner roll chapter; enriched white rolls baked in a batch for the table. Mrs Rundell, A New System of Domestic Cookery (1806), Project Gutenberg: the dinner roll as a staple of Victorian table service.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled in a bag for up to 2 months. Reheat from frozen at 160°C for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the day before; reheat briefly in a warm oven before serving.

content.text: Pull-apart rolls baked touching in a tin come out with soft, fluffy sides where the dough pieces met and a golden crust on top. The enrichment is modest (milk and a little butter), which keeps the rolls light and gives them a longer shelf life than a plain water roll. They work equally well at a Sunday dinner table or packed into lunchboxes the next day.
Mix, knead, and bulk ferment, 1 hour

Mix {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{salt-table}}, and {{caster-sugar}}. Add the {{whole-milk}}, {{water}}, and {{unsalted-butter}}. Bring together and knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Cover and leave until doubled, about 1 hour.

Line a rectangular tin (roughly 30×20 cm) with baking paper. Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces. Shape each into a tight ball and arrange in a 3×4 grid in the tin. They should fit snugly. Cover and prove for 40 minutes until visibly risen and touching. Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan).

Brush gently with milk. Bake for 16–18 minutes until golden on top. The centres should be cooked through — lift the tin and check the base is pale gold, not pale and wet. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack. Pull apart to serve.
